title: The Missing Manifold: Quantum Theory's Deepest Structural Hole

source.original: What Is Quantum Mechanics Really Telling Us? — David Deutsch & Brian Greene | World Science Festival

body_html:
General relativity has a beautiful architectural feature that quantum mechanics conspicuously lacks. Einstein's field equations don't just float in abstraction — they describe the behavior of a specific mathematical object: a four-dimensional pseudo-Riemannian manifold equipped with a metric. That manifold is spacetime. You can point to it and say, "This is the thing whose properties the equations govern." David Deutsch argues that quantum mechanics has no equivalent, and that this absence is the deepest open problem facing the Everettian program.

In the Everettian picture, we speak freely about the multiverse — parallel universes that are approximately autonomous, occasionally interfering with one another in experiments, and branching whenever decoherence suppresses their mutual interactions. But there is no mathematical object in the formalism that is the multiverse. There is Hilbert space, there are observables, there are equations of motion — the analogs of Einstein's equations — but nothing analogous to the manifold from which the approximate decomposition into quasi-classical worlds can be rigorously derived.

Deutsch is precise about what is missing: you cannot have a well-defined approximation without knowing what it is an approximation to. The familiar story of decoherence — where environmental interactions suppress interference and produce an apparent classical world — is an approximation scheme. But the exact structure it approximates has not been specified. Until it is, the many-worlds interpretation rests on a foundation that, while physically motivated, is mathematically incomplete.

Some recent work in quantum gravity and string theory hints at progress — researchers have explored how spatial geometry can emerge from entanglement structure in a boundary theory — but Deutsch views these developments as still far from delivering the needed object. He considers this problem categorically harder than the probability question, which he regards as solved through the decision-theoretic derivation of the Born rule. Finding the multiverse's manifold, he suggests, may require entirely new mathematical ideas, perhaps emerging from the constructor-theoretic framework that replaces dynamical laws with statements about possible and impossible transformations.

The stakes are enormous. Without the missing manifold, Everettian quantum mechanics can describe what happens and predict outcomes, but it cannot fully articulate what exists. And for a theory whose founding motivation is realism — taking the mathematics seriously as a description of reality — that gap is not a footnote. It is the central unfinished business of fundamental physics.
